lesson_title: std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.back: std::vector<T>::size_type, which is typically std::size_t (an unsigned integer type).
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.text: std::size_t is an {{c1::unsigned}} integer type, typically used for array indexing and loop counting.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.answer: Infinite loop. When i is 0 and decremented, it wraps around to the maximum unsigned value (not -1).
cards.explanation: Unsigned integers cannot go negative. i >= 0 is always true for unsigned types, causing an infinite loop.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.concept: Why avoid unsigned integers for loop counters and indices
cards.explanation:
Unsigned integers cause problems:
  • Wraparound behavior when going below 0 (underflow)
  • Comparisons like i >= 0 are always true
  • Mixing signed and unsigned causes unexpected conversions
  • Difficult to catch off-by-one errors
LearnCPP recommends using signed integers (like int) for indices and counters.
examples:
// Bad: unsigned can wrap
for (std::size_t i{n-1}; i >= 0; --i)

// Good: use signed int
for (int i{n-1}; i >= 0; --i)
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.front: What does LearnCPP recommend using for vector indices instead of std::size_t?
cards.back: Signed integers like int or std::ptrdiff_t. This avoids wraparound issues and makes code safer and more intuitive.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.text: To get the length of a vector as a signed integer, use {{c1::std::ssize()}} (C++20) or {{c2::static_cast<int>(vec.size())}}.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.question: How would you rewrite this safely?
cards.answer:
std::vector<int> v{1, 2, 3};
for (int i{0}; i < std::ssize(v); ++i)
    std::cout << v[i];
cards.explanation: Use signed int for the index and std::ssize() to get signed length, avoiding signed/unsigned comparison warnings.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.front: What happens when you compare a signed int with an unsigned std::size_t?
cards.back: The signed int is implicitly converted to unsigned. If the signed value is negative, it converts to a large unsigned value, causing unexpected comparison results.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.text: The {{c1::std::ssize()}} function (C++20) returns the size of a container as a {{c2::signed}} integer type.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.back: Signed integer type typically used for pointer differences and array indices
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.question: What will this output if vec is empty?
cards.answer: A very large number (like 18446744073709551615 on 64-bit systems)
cards.explanation: vec.size() returns 0 (unsigned). Subtracting 1 from unsigned 0 wraps around to the maximum unsigned value.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

cards.front: Why is std::size_t problematic when used for loop counters that decrement?
cards.back: Because unsigned values cannot be negative. When decrementing from 0, they wrap to the maximum unsigned value instead of going to -1, causing infinite loops or unexpected behavior.
cards.source: 16.3 - std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem
